q: Heat transfer is a discipline of thermal engineering that concerns the generation, use, conversion, and exchange of thermal energy between physical systems.
The presence of a temperature difference is one of the basic requirements for heat transfer.
Net heat transfer can occur between two systems that are at the same temperature.
In fluids, heat is mainly transferred by conduction process.
The individual parts of a medium move as a whole during the process of heat conduction.
In heat conduction, heat energy is transferred by successive distribution among the adjacent parts.
Heat energy transferred internally via conduction involves the vibrations of atoms and molecules.
Metals are generally poor conductors of heat because they have very few free electrons.
The thermal conductivity of a material is a measure of its ability to conduct heat.
The ratio $\frac{T_2 - T_1}{l}$ is called the thermal resistance gradient.
Fourier's law states that the rate of heat flow per unit area is proportional to the magnitude of temperature gradient.
A negative sign in the differential form of Fourier's law indicates that temperature $T$ must increase as distance $x$ increases.
The SI unit for heat flux density is watt per metre squared ($W \cdot m^{-2}$).
A low value for thermal conductivity indicates that the material is a good thermal conductor.
Materials that are good electric conductors, such as copper and silver, are also good heat conductors.
Heat convection is the flow of heat through a fluid from places of higher temperature to places of lower temperature by movement of the fluid itself.
In heat convection, the transfer of energy occurs due to fluid motion.
Convection currents are set up in a fluid because the hotter part of the fluid is denser than the cooler part.
Convection occurs only in a liquid or a gas, never in a solid.
Buoyant force is what drives natural convection.
Newton's law of cooling is the basic equation for the rate of radiation heat transfer.
When $T_2 < T_1$ in the convection equation, heat is lost by the object and $H_{conv}$ is negative.
Heat radiation is a transfer of heat that requires a dense material medium to propagate.
Energy transfer by radiation is the fastest mode of heat transfer, traveling at the speed of light.
Energy transfer by radiation suffers no attenuation in a vacuum.
All bodies at a temperature above absolute zero emit thermal radiation.
Thermal radiation is identical to electromagnetic radiation such as X-rays and gamma rays in its relationship to temperature.
A black object is a poor absorber and a poor radiator of thermal energy.
The emissivity of a black object is close to 1.
A white or silver coated object is a poor absorber and a poor radiator whose emissivity is much less than 1.
A shiny object may reflect a great deal of visible light but still be a good absorber of infrared light.
Stefan-Boltzmann law states that the rate of heat radiation is inversely proportional to the fourth power of the absolute temperature.
A blackbody is an object which can absorb all the electromagnetic radiations falling upon it.
